pattern: Because using a column locks out every column to its left for every word, the only thing that matters at each step is how many characters are still available going forward -- a column-by-column knapsack over how much of the target has been placed so far.
hints: Every word has the same length, and using column k in any word permanently retires columns 0..k for all words -- so think of processing the shared column index from left to right exactly once.
For a fixed column, precompute how many words have each letter at that column; that count is exactly how many ways you can use this column to place one particular target character next.

Track, for each number of target characters already placed, how many ways reach that state; when you consider the next column you can either skip it entirely or use it to place the next needed target character, so update the counts for 'already placed i characters' from the smaller indices upward and take everything modulo 1e9+7.

edge_inputs.description: single word, no matching character anywhere
target longer than every word, so no arrangement of columns can possibly cover it
every word identical, still each column only usable once overall
target requires the same character repeated more times than columns can provide without reuse
many words share the needed letters, producing a large combinatorial count

stress.description: random small alphabet words and target so many column choices overlap
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    num_words = max(1, min(size, 40))
    word_len = max(1, min(size // 2 + 1, 25))
    target_len = max(1, min(word_len, 12))
    alphabet = 'abc'
    words = [''.join(rng.choice(alphabet) for _ in range(word_len)) for _ in range(num_words)]
    target = ''.join(rng.choice(alphabet) for _ in range(target_len))
    return [words, target]
